coefficients.label: Nile tilapia feed conversion ratio
sources: Shaw, Knopf & Kloas (2022), Sustainability 14(7):4064, DOI 10.3390/su14074064 (FCR 0.86–1.79 across protein sources)
Rodde et al. (2020), Aquaculture Reports 18:100349, DOI 10.1016/j.aqrep.2020.100349 (GIFT strain, individual rearing)
coefficients.note: Strongly diet-dependent; well-run commercial RAS ~1.4–1.8. Seed 1.7 is high-but-valid.

coefficients.label: Catfish feed conversion ratio
sources: African catfish (Clarias gariepinus) commercial RAS FCR ~0.8–1.0
coefficients.note: SPECIES AMBIGUITY: 'catfish' spans efficient Clarias (~0.8–1.0) and channel catfish (~1.5–2.0). Seed 1.6 implies channel catfish — confirm which you stock, as it nearly doubles the implied biomass for the same feed.

coefficients.label: Rainbow trout feed conversion ratio
sources: Rainbow trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss) literature FCR ~0.8–1.5 (trials report 0.99–1.49)

coefficients.label: Lettuce feeding-rate ratio
sources: Rakocy/UVI raft guideline: 60–100 g/m²/day for leafy greens (lettuce originally 60 g, Bibb lettuce, 1988)
coefficients.note: Load-bearing sizing rule. Seed 60 sits at the conservative (low) end of the UVI band.

coefficients.label: Basil feeding-rate ratio
sources: Rakocy, Shultz, Bailey & Thoman (2004), 'Aquaponic production of tilapia and basil', Acta Hort. 648:63–69 (measured 81.4 g/m²/day batch, 99.6 staggered)
coefficients.note: Recalibrated from the earlier 70 g/m²/day stub to 85 — mid the UVI-measured basil band (81–100, Rakocy et al. 2004) — so a basil system is no longer under-fed.

coefficients.label: Tomato (fruiting) feeding-rate ratio
sources: Somerville et al. (2014), FAO 589: fruiting raft ~100+ g/m²/day
coefficients.note: Fruiting crops carry a higher feed load than leafy. Seed 110 is mid-band.

coefficients.label: Lettuce edible yield (annualized)
sources: DWC aquaponic lettuce ~1.0–1.42 kg/m²/cycle (AUC thesis, fount.aucegypt.edu/etds/795); ~8–11 cycles/yr
coefficients.note: Per-cycle yields annualized — depends heavily on cycles/year (climate, cultivar). Seed 25 is optimistic-but-plausible for year-round warm-climate production.

coefficients.label: Nile tilapia harvest weight
sources: Common small-scale/aquaponic tilapia harvest target 0.4–0.6 kg (Somerville et al. 2014, FAO 589); commercial up to ~0.8 kg
IoTPond open dataset (data/empirical_envelope.json): observed max ~0.39 kg over a Jun–Oct grow-out at ~24.5 °C (below the 27–30 °C optimum)
coefficients.note: Seed 0.5 kg is a reasonable target, but the real ponds we ingested only reached ~0.39 kg — a sub-optimal-temperature grow-out finishes lighter than the target.
